question_title: Will the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX) close at or above 31,000 on June 12, 2026?
summary: NDX closed at 28,957.60 on June 5 after a massive 4.77% single-day crash driven by a much hotter-than-expected jobs report (172K vs 80K), spiking Treasury yields (10Y above 4.5%), and a violent semiconductor selloff (semi ETF -10%). To reach 31,000 by June 12, NDX needs a 7.05% gain in just 5 trading days — an extreme move. The crash catalysts are fundamental and persistent: elevated yields suppress tech valuations, and the SpaceX $75B mega-IPO on the exact resolution date creates additional capital rotation pressure away from existing Nasdaq names. While some post-crash mean reversion is possible, a Monte Carlo mixture model with elevated post-crash volatility across multiple scenarios yields approximately 5% probability of reaching 31,000.

factors.description: NDX crashed 4.77% on June 5 to 28,957.60 — now needs 7.05% gain in just 5 trading days to reach 31,000, which is an extreme move even in volatile markets
Crash driven by fundamental headwinds that persist: hot jobs report (172K vs 80K expected) pushed 10Y yield above 4.5%, and semiconductor ETF dropped 10% on Broadcom AI guidance disappointment
SpaceX IPO on June 12 (same resolution date) creating capital rotation pressure — largest IPO in history at $75B raise draining liquidity from existing tech/chip names
Post-crash mean reversion tendency — markets often partially bounce after sharp selloffs, and semi ETF still up 79% YTD suggests no fundamental crisis
AI/semiconductor supercycle remains intact (SK Hynix +169%, memory prices +60% in Q1) — crash may be temporary rotation rather than end of trend
Monte Carlo mixture model combining four scenarios (continuation, partial bounce, strong recovery, continued selloff) with post-crash elevated volatility yields P(>=31000) = 4.82%

reasoning:
**Forecast: 5% probability (YES)**

### Key findings:

1. **Critical June 5 crash**: NDX dropped 4.77% to 28,957.60 — its worst day since the April 2025 tariff turmoil. This moved the goalpost dramatically: the index now needs a **7.05% gain in just 5 trading days** to reach 31,000.

2. **Fundamental drivers of the crash** (not transient noise):
   - Much hotter-than-expected jobs report (172K vs 80K forecast)
   - 10-year Treasury yield spiked above 4.5%, 30-year above 5%
   - Semiconductor ETF dropped 10% (Broadcom -8%, Marvell -16%, Micron -13%)
   - Capital rotation ahead of SpaceX's historic $75B IPO on June 12

3. **Monte Carlo simulation**: A mixture model combining four scenarios (status quo continuation, partial bounce, strong recovery, continued selling) with elevated post-crash volatility produced P(≥31,000) = **4.82%**

4. **Why not lower**: Some mean reversion is possible after a ~5% crash, the AI supercycle remains intact (semi ETF still +79% YTD), and elevated post-crash volatility creates wider distributions

5. **Why not higher**: 7%+ weekly gains in NDX are historically extremely rare, the crash drivers are structural (yields, rotation), and SpaceX IPO on the exact resolution date adds selling pressure on existing tech names
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX) close at or above 31,000 on June 12, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43784)
[Nasdaq falls 4% and suffers worst day since April 2025 as traders flee chip stocks: Live updates](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/06/04/stock-market-today-live-updates.html)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves **YES** if the official closing value of the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX) on Friday, June 12, 2026 is at or above 31,000.00, as reported on the [Nasdaq official index history page for NDX](https://indexes.nasdaq.com/Index/History/NDX). It resolves **NO** otherwise (including if NDX closes below 31,000.00).

If the Nasdaq official page is unavailable, the [Yahoo Finance ^NDX historical data page](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/%5ENDX/history/) will be used as the fallback source. If June 12, 2026 is unexpectedly not a trading day (e.g., due to an unscheduled market closure), the closing value of the most recent prior trading day in the week of June 8–12, 2026 will be used.
fine_print: If both primary and fallback sources are unavailable through June 14, 2026, any [credible source](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) reporting the official NDX close for June 12, 2026 may be used.

factors.description: NDX crashed 4.77% on June 5 to 28,957.60, now needs 7.05% gain in 5 trading days to reach 31,000 - an extreme move
Crash driven by fundamental factors: hot jobs report (172K vs 80K expected), 10Y yield above 4.5%, chip selloff (semiconductor ETF -10%) - these headwinds persist
SpaceX IPO on June 12 (same day as resolution) creating capital rotation away from existing tech/chip stocks into the largest IPO in history ($75B raise)
Post-crash mean reversion tendency - markets often partially bounce after sharp selloffs, especially when semi ETF still up 79% YTD suggesting no fundamental crisis
AI/semiconductor supercycle remains intact (SK Hynix +169%, memory prices +60%) - the crash may be a temporary rotation, not end of trend
Monte Carlo mixture model with elevated post-crash volatility yields P(>=31000) of ~5% across reasonable parameter assumptions

input.anchor: Historical base rate for NDX gaining 7%+ in 5 trading days: extremely rare, roughly 1-3% probability in normal environments, somewhat higher (~5-8%) in high-volatility post-crash environments with mean reversion
input.assessment:
NDX needs to gain 7.05% in 5 trading days after a massive 4.77% single-day crash on June 5. The crash was fundamentally driven by: (1) much hotter-than-expected jobs data pushing yields sharply higher, (2) semiconductor selloff (Broadcom disappointing on AI chip outlook), and (3) portfolio rotation ahead of SpaceX's $75B IPO on June 12. These headwinds are structural and persist into next week.

The Monte Carlo mixture model combining four scenarios (continuation, partial bounce, strong recovery, continued selloff) with weights reflecting the fundamental backdrop yields approximately 5% probability. The P95 of the final price distribution (~30,979) is just barely below the 31,000 target, confirming this is a ~5th percentile outcome.

Key tension: Post-crash mean reversion could provide some bounce, but the required magnitude (7%+) is extreme, and the catalysts for the crash are persistent rather than transient. The SpaceX IPO on the exact resolution date adds selling pressure on existing tech names.
input.calibration_notes: Base rate: 7%+ weekly gain in NDX is extremely rare historically. Even with 30% annualized vol and zero drift, the probability is only ~5%. The post-crash environment might add 1-3% via mean reversion, but the fundamental headwinds (yields, rotation) offset this. The 5% estimate aligns well with both simulation output and historical base rates.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether the June 5 crash triggers forced selling (margin calls, risk parity deleveraging) that continues into next week, or whether it creates a buying opportunity. 2. Whether the SpaceX IPO excitement lifts sentiment broadly or just drains capital from existing names. 3. Whether there's any Fed or policy surprise that could shift yields significantly lower in the next week.
input.tool_audit: FRED API: Excellent - provided full daily NDX data through June 4 with regime statistics. Nasdaq official page: Confirmed June 5 close at 28,957.60. CNBC news article: Provided clear explanation of crash causes. Research sub-agent: Good macro context but Google Finance price was from earlier in the day. Yahoo Finance: Stale data (May 20) - temporary issues. Monte Carlo simulations: Successfully modeled multiple scenarios.
input.update_triggers: 1. NDX opens strongly on Monday June 8 (above 29,500) suggesting V-shaped recovery. 2. Fed emergency communication or dovish signal that pushes yields down. 3. Additional semiconductor/tech bad news extending the selloff. 4. SpaceX IPO postponed or scaled back.
input.process_reflection: The process worked well for this question. FRED was the most valuable data source, providing precise daily NDX closes. The critical insight came from detecting the June 5 crash via the Nasdaq official page and web search, which completely changed the forecast from ~30-40% (when I was working from June 4 close) to ~5%. The research sub-agent provided excellent macro context. One friction point: Yahoo Finance data was stale, which could have been misleading without the FRED fallback.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
**Verification Summary:**

All six factors are well-grounded in the research trace:

1. **NDX crash to 28,957.60 (-4.77%)** — Confirmed by Nasdaq official page snippet ("28,957.60 -1,434.15 -4.77%") and cross-validated against FRED June 4 close of 30,407.81. The 7.05% required gain and 5 trading days remaining are confirmed by execute_code output.

2. **Crash causes (172K jobs vs 80K expected, 10Y >4.5%, semi ETF -10%)** — All three data points confirmed by CNBC article fetch: "Nonfarm payrolls increased by 172,000 in May...well above the expected 80,000", "10-year yield jumped above 4.5%", "iShares Semiconductor ETF dropped 10%".

3. **SpaceX IPO June 12** — Confirmed by research sub-agent (SpaceX IPO June 12, ticker SPCX, ~$75B raise) and CNBC ("analysts believe investors are selling chip/tech stocks to make room for SpaceX IPO").

4. **Semi ETF up 79% YTD** — Directly from CNBC: "iShares Semiconductor ETF is still up 79% year-to-date."

5. **SK Hynix +169%, memory +60%** — Confirmed in research sub-agent output and worldview research entry.

6. **Monte Carlo ~5%** — Final simulation output: "WEIGHTED MIXTURE PROBABILITY: P(NDX >= 31,000 on June 12) = 4.82%"

**Double-counting noted but handled:** The agent acknowledges that Factor 1 (distance to target) and Factor 6 (Monte Carlo) overlap, and that the factor-sum-implied probability (~0.8%) is artificially low. The agent correctly relies on the Monte Carlo mixture model as the primary basis rather than mechanically applying the logit sum. This is methodologically sound.

**Resolution criteria alignment:** The agent fetched the actual resolution source (Nasdaq official index history page) and correctly identified the current price relative to the 31,000 threshold. All factors engage with the specific target and timeframe.

**Probability assessment:** The 5% estimate is consistent with the Monte Carlo mixture model (4.82%) and sits within the stated anchor range for post-crash environments (5-8%). The mixture model weights (60% continuation, 25% partial bounce, 10% strong recovery, 5% continued selloff) are reasonable given fundamentally-driven crash catalysts. My independent estimate would be 3-7%, fully consistent with 5%.

**Counterargument quality:** Substantive — constructs a specific V-shaped recovery narrative with mechanisms, historical parallels, and quantitative triggers for updating. Not a token gesture.

**Confidence (0.55):** Appropriate given strong quantitative basis but genuine uncertainty around post-crash drift parameters.

**Tool audit:** Consistent with ground-truth metrics (18 calls, 0 errors). No false claims.

No errors found.
